system-prompt.role: You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.

field.name: type
field.option: Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity
Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony
Mexican — Bold, layered flavors built on chiles, corn, and slow-cooked techniques

field.name: restriction
field.option: Vegetarian — No meat or fish, but allows dairy and eggs
Gluten-Free — No wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives
Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products

field.name: method
field.option: Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat
Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results
Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor

rules.rule: Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments.

Plan before you build — call tasklist(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones with optional dependsOn for DAG dependencies, then call completeTask(tasklistId, taskId, output) or completeTaskAsync(tasklistId, taskId, fn) as you complete each one.
Await every async call: var x = await fn()
Use stop() to read runtime values before branching.
Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values.
Do not import modules. Do not use export.
Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns.
Handle nullability with ?. and ??
After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response.

Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Check the Knowledge Tree to see what is available. NEVER load all files from a domain or space — only select the specific options that are relevant to the user's request. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.

system-prompt.instructions:
You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients, dietary guidelines, food database, and meal scoring
When the user asks for help:
1. Start with `ask()` using a form component to gather their request 2. Use `loadKnowledge()` to load relevant knowledge from BOTH spaces as needed 3. Read the loaded content with `await stop()` to understand it 4. Use the utility functions (estimateCalories, scaleIngredients, buildGroceryList, formatTime) to compute values 5. Display results using the display components (RecipeCard, NutritionCard, TipCard, MealPlanCard)
Make sure to add a tasklist for using all the available functions. Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.
When a recipe needs nutritional analysis, spawn the `diet_analyst` agent from the nutrition space to handle it. It has its own knowledge base, scoring functions, and display components.
## Pre-loaded Knowledge
#### cuisine/type/italian # Italian Cuisine
Italian cooking centers on high-quality, seasonal ingredients prepared simply to let flavors shine.
## Key Principles - **Simplicity** — Most dishes use 4–8 ingredients. The quality of each ingredient matters more than complexity. - **Regionality** — Northern Italy favors butter, rice, and polenta; Southern Italy uses olive oil, tomatoes, and dried pasta. - **Al dente** — Pasta should be cooked firm to the bite. Start tasting 2 minutes before the package time. - **Soffritto** — The holy trinity of onion, carrot, and celery, slowly sweated in olive oil, forms the base of countless dishes.
## Essential Techniques - **Emulsifying pasta water** — Reserve starchy pasta water and toss with sauce to create a silky coating. - **Risotto stirring** — Constant stirring releases starch for a creamy texture without cream. - **Bruschetta method** — Rub toasted bread with raw garlic, then drizzle with good olive oil.
## Staple Ingredients Olive oil, San Marzano tomatoes, Parmigiano-Reggiano, fresh basil, garlic, dried pasta, arborio rice, prosciutto, mozzarella.
## Classic Dishes Cacio e pepe, carbonara, margherita pizza, risotto alla milanese, osso buco, tiramisu.
## Slash Actions
/mealplan — Make a meal plan Do a configurable meal plan Steps: Define Meal Preferences → Design Weekly Menu → Create Prep Schedule → Build Grocery List /recipe — Create a recipe Generate a complete recipe with nutrition analysis Steps: Select Cuisine and Dish → Gather Ingredients → Prepare Mise en Place → Cook Step by Step → Analyze Nutrition /technique — Learn a technique Learn a cooking technique step-by-step Steps: Identify Technique → Explain Core Principles → Practice Exercises → Reference Recipe

system-prompt.role: You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.

field.name: type
field.option: Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity
Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony
Mexican — Bold, layered flavors built on chiles, corn, and slow-cooked techniques

field.name: restriction
field.option: Vegetarian — No meat or fish, but allows dairy and eggs
Gluten-Free — No wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives
Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products

field.name: method
field.option: Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat
Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results
Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor

rules.rule: Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments.

Plan before you build — call tasklist(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones with optional dependsOn for DAG dependencies, then call completeTask(tasklistId, taskId, output) or completeTaskAsync(tasklistId, taskId, fn) as you complete each one.
Await every async call: var x = await fn()
Use stop() to read runtime values before branching.
Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values.
Do not import modules. Do not use export.
Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns.
Handle nullability with ?. and ??
After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response.

Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Check the Knowledge Tree to see what is available. NEVER load all files from a domain or space — only select the specific options that are relevant to the user's request. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.

system-prompt.instructions:
You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients, dietary guidelines, food database, and meal scoring
When the user asks for help:
1. Start with `ask()` using a form component to gather their request 2. Use `loadKnowledge()` to load relevant knowledge from BOTH spaces as needed 3. Read the loaded content with `await stop()` to understand it 4. Use the utility functions (estimateCalories, scaleIngredients, buildGroceryList, formatTime) to compute values 5. Display results using the display components (RecipeCard, NutritionCard, TipCard, MealPlanCard)
Make sure to add a tasklist for using all the available functions. Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.
When a recipe needs nutritional analysis, spawn the `diet_analyst` agent from the nutrition space to handle it. It has its own knowledge base, scoring functions, and display components.
## Pre-loaded Knowledge
#### cuisine/type/italian # Italian Cuisine
Italian cooking centers on high-quality, seasonal ingredients prepared simply to let flavors shine.
## Key Principles - **Simplicity** — Most dishes use 4–8 ingredients. The quality of each ingredient matters more than complexity. - **Regionality** — Northern Italy favors butter, rice, and polenta; Southern Italy uses olive oil, tomatoes, and dried pasta. - **Al dente** — Pasta should be cooked firm to the bite. Start tasting 2 minutes before the package time. - **Soffritto** — The holy trinity of onion, carrot, and celery, slowly sweated in olive oil, forms the base of countless dishes.
## Essential Techniques - **Emulsifying pasta water** — Reserve starchy pasta water and toss with sauce to create a silky coating. - **Risotto stirring** — Constant stirring releases starch for a creamy texture without cream. - **Bruschetta method** — Rub toasted bread with raw garlic, then drizzle with good olive oil.
## Staple Ingredients Olive oil, San Marzano tomatoes, Parmigiano-Reggiano, fresh basil, garlic, dried pasta, arborio rice, prosciutto, mozzarella.
## Classic Dishes Cacio e pepe, carbonara, margherita pizza, risotto alla milanese, osso buco, tiramisu.
## Slash Actions
/mealplan — Make a meal plan Do a configurable meal plan Steps: Define Meal Preferences → Design Weekly Menu → Create Prep Schedule → Build Grocery List /recipe — Create a recipe Generate a complete recipe with nutrition analysis Steps: Select Cuisine and Dish → Gather Ingredients → Prepare Mise en Place → Cook Step by Step → Analyze Nutrition /technique — Learn a technique Learn a cooking technique step-by-step Steps: Identify Technique → Explain Core Principles → Practice Exercises → Reference Recipe

debug-log.message: a pasta for four person vegetarian

system-prompt.role: You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.

field.name: type
field.option: Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity
Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony
Mexican — Bold, layered flavors built on chiles, corn, and slow-cooked techniques

field.name: restriction
field.option: Vegetarian — No meat or fish, but allows dairy and eggs
Gluten-Free — No wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives
Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products

field.name: method
field.option: Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat
Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results
Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor

rules.rule: Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments.

Plan before you build — call tasklist(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones with optional dependsOn for DAG dependencies, then call completeTask(tasklistId, taskId, output) or completeTaskAsync(tasklistId, taskId, fn) as you complete each one.
Await every async call: var x = await fn()
Use stop() to read runtime values before branching.
Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values.
Do not import modules. Do not use export.
Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns.
Handle nullability with ?. and ??
After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response.

Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Check the Knowledge Tree to see what is available. NEVER load all files from a domain or space — only select the specific options that are relevant to the user's request. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.

system-prompt.instructions:
You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients, dietary guidelines, food database, and meal scoring
When the user asks for help:
1. Start with `ask()` using a form component to gather their request 2. Use `loadKnowledge()` to load relevant knowledge from BOTH spaces as needed 3. Read the loaded content with `await stop()` to understand it 4. Use the utility functions (estimateCalories, scaleIngredients, buildGroceryList, formatTime) to compute values 5. Display results using the display components (RecipeCard, NutritionCard, TipCard, MealPlanCard)
Make sure to add a tasklist for using all the available functions. Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.
When a recipe needs nutritional analysis, spawn the `diet_analyst` agent from the nutrition space to handle it. It has its own knowledge base, scoring functions, and display components.
## Pre-loaded Knowledge
#### cuisine/type/italian # Italian Cuisine
Italian cooking centers on high-quality, seasonal ingredients prepared simply to let flavors shine.
## Key Principles - **Simplicity** — Most dishes use 4–8 ingredients. The quality of each ingredient matters more than complexity. - **Regionality** — Northern Italy favors butter, rice, and polenta; Southern Italy uses olive oil, tomatoes, and dried pasta. - **Al dente** — Pasta should be cooked firm to the bite. Start tasting 2 minutes before the package time. - **Soffritto** — The holy trinity of onion, carrot, and celery, slowly sweated in olive oil, forms the base of countless dishes.
## Essential Techniques - **Emulsifying pasta water** — Reserve starchy pasta water and toss with sauce to create a silky coating. - **Risotto stirring** — Constant stirring releases starch for a creamy texture without cream. - **Bruschetta method** — Rub toasted bread with raw garlic, then drizzle with good olive oil.
## Staple Ingredients Olive oil, San Marzano tomatoes, Parmigiano-Reggiano, fresh basil, garlic, dried pasta, arborio rice, prosciutto, mozzarella.
## Classic Dishes Cacio e pepe, carbonara, margherita pizza, risotto alla milanese, osso buco, tiramisu.
## Slash Actions
/mealplan — Make a meal plan Do a configurable meal plan Steps: Define Meal Preferences → Design Weekly Menu → Create Prep Schedule → Build Grocery List /recipe — Create a recipe Generate a complete recipe with nutrition analysis Steps: Select Cuisine and Dish → Gather Ingredients → Prepare Mise en Place → Cook Step by Step → Analyze Nutrition /technique — Learn a technique Learn a cooking technique step-by-step Steps: Identify Technique → Explain Core Principles → Practice Exercises → Reference Recipe

turn.message:
← stop { loaded: { "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "italian": "# Italian Cuisine

Italian cooking centers on high-quality, seasonal ingredients prepared simply to let flavors shine.

## Key Principles
- **Simplicity** — Most dishes use 4–8 ingredients. The quality of each ingredient matters more than complexity.
- **Regionality** — Northern Italy favors butter, rice, and polenta; Southern Italy uses olive oil, tomatoes, and dried pasta.
- **Al dente** — Pasta should be cooked firm to the bite. Start tasting 2 minutes before the package time.
- **Soffritto** — The holy trinity of onion, carrot, and celery, slowly sweated in olive oil, forms the base of countless dishes.

## Essential Techniques
- **Emulsifying pasta water** — Reserve starchy pasta water and toss with sauce to create a silky coating.
- **Risotto stirring** — Constant stirring releases starch for a creamy texture without cream.
- **Bruschetta method** — Rub toasted bread with raw garlic, then drizzle with good olive oil.

## Staple Ingredients
Olive oil, San Marzano tomatoes, Parmigiano-Reggiano, fresh basil, garlic, dried pasta, arborio rice, prosciutto, mozzarella.

## Classic Dishes
Cacio e pepe, carbonara, margherita pizza, risotto alla milanese, osso buco, tiramisu." } }, "dietary": { "restriction": { "vegetarian": "# Vegetarian Cooking

Vegetarian cooking excludes meat and fish but includes dairy, eggs, and honey.

## Substitution Strategies
- **Protein sources** — Eggs, cheese, legumes (lentils, chickpeas, black beans), tofu, tempeh, seitan, nuts
- **Umami replacements** — Soy sauce, miso paste, nutritional yeast, aged cheeses, mushrooms, tomato paste
- **Stock** — Use vegetable stock or mushroom stock instead of chicken/beef stock
- **Gelatin** — Replace with agar-agar (1:1 ratio, but boil to activate)

## Tips
- Roast vegetables at high heat (220°C+) for caramelization and depth
- Use smoked paprika, liquid smoke, or charred vegetables for smoky notes usually provided by meat
- Don't treat vegetarian food as "meat minus the meat" — build dishes around vegetables as the star", "gluten-free": "# Gluten-Free Cooking

Gluten-free cooking avoids wheat, barley, rye, and products derived from them. This is essential for people with celiac disease or gluten sensitivity.

## Substitution Strategies
- **Flour** — Rice flour, almond flour, oat flour (certified GF), tapioca starch, potato starch, or commercial GF blends
- **Pasta** — Rice noodles, corn pasta, chickpea pasta, zucchini noodles, sweet potato glass noodles
- **Breadcrumbs** — Crushed rice crackers, almond meal, crushed cornflakes, polenta crumbs
- **Thickeners** — Cornstarch or arrowroot instead of wheat flour for sauces and gravies
- **Soy sauce** — Use tamari (check label for wheat-free) or coconut aminos

## Hidden Gluten Sources
- Soy sauce (most brands contain wheat)
- Malt vinegar
- Some spice blends and bouillon cubes
- Beer (use GF beer or wine for cooking)
- Processed foods with "modified food starch"

## Tips
- Many cuisines are naturally gluten-free: Mexican (corn-based), Japanese (rice-based), Indian (lentil and rice dishes)
- When baking GF, add xanthan gum (½ tsp per cup of flour) to improve structure
- Always check labels — gluten hides in unexpected places", "dairy-free": "# Dairy-Free Cooking

Dairy-free cooking eliminates milk, cheese, butter, cream, yogurt, and all products containing them.

## Substitution Strategies
- **Butter** — Olive oil, coconut oil, or vegan butter. For baking, use coconut oil (solid) at a 1:1 ratio.
- **Milk** — Oat milk (best for cooking/baking), coconut milk (rich sauces, curries), almond milk (light uses)
- **Cream** — Full-fat coconut cream, cashew cream (blend soaked cashews with water)
- **Cheese** — Nutritional yeast for cheesy flavor, cashew-based sauces for creaminess, avocado for richness
- **Yogurt** — Coconut yogurt or soy yogurt

## Tips
- Coconut cream is the best dairy-free substitute for heavy cream in sauces — refrigerate the can and scoop the solid cream
- Nutritional yeast adds a savory, cheese-like flavor to pasta, popcorn, and sauces
- Many Asian cuisines (Thai, Chinese, Japanese) are naturally dairy-free and excellent references
- When making a roux, use olive oil or vegan butter instead of butter — the technique is identical" } }, "technique": { "method": { "saute": "# Sauté

High-heat, quick cooking in a wide pan with a small amount of fat. The word comes from French "sauter" — to jump.

## When to Use
- Vegetables that should stay crisp-tender (bell peppers, snap peas, asparagus)
- Thin cuts of meat or fish (chicken cutlets, shrimp, scallops)
- Finishing a dish with a pan sauce after searing

## Key Technique
1. **Heat the pan first**, then add oil. The oil should shimmer but not smoke.
2. **Don't overcrowd** — ingredients should sit in a single layer with space between them. Work in batches if needed.
3. **Keep things moving** — toss or stir frequently. The goal is even browning without burning.
4. **Season at the end** — salt draws out moisture and can prevent browning.

## Equipment
A wide, flat-bottomed skillet or sauté pan. Stainless steel gives the best fond (browned bits) for pan sauces. Non-stick works for delicate items like fish.

## Common Mistakes
- Starting with a cold pan (food steams instead of searing)
- Adding too much food at once (temperature drops, food boils)
- Moving food too often when you want a sear (let it sit for 2–3 minutes)", "braise": "# Braise

Braising combines dry heat (searing) and wet heat (simmering in liquid) for dishes that are deeply flavorful and fall-apart tender.

## When to Use
- Tough, collagen-rich cuts of meat (short ribs, pork shoulder, lamb shanks, chuck roast)
- Root vegetables that benefit from long cooking (carrots, turnips, parsnips)
- Any dish where you want rich, concentrated sauce built from the cooking liquid

## Key Technique
1. **Sear first** — Pat the protein dry, season generously, and sear in hot oil until deeply browned on all sides. This is where most of the flavor comes from.
2. **Build aromatics** — Sauté onions, carrots, celery, and garlic in the same pot after searing.
3. **Deglaze** — Add wine, stock, or other liquid and scrape up the fond (browned bits).
4. **Submerge halfway** — The liquid should come about halfway up the protein, not cover it completely.
5. **Low and slow** — Cover and cook at 150–165°C (300–325°F) for 2–4 hours until fork-tender.
6. **Rest and reduce** — Remove the protein, strain and reduce the braising liquid into a sauce.

## Equipment
A heavy Dutch oven with a tight-fitting lid. Cast iron or enameled cast iron retains heat evenly.

## Common Mistakes
- Skipping the sear (you lose enormous flavor)
- Cooking at too high a temperature (the protein toughens before the collagen breaks down)
- Not reducing the braising liquid (thin, watery sauce instead of a rich glaze)", "grill": "# Grill

Grilling uses direct radiant heat from below to cook food quickly, creating characteristic char marks and smoky flavor.

## When to Use
- Steaks, burgers, chops, and other quick-cooking proteins
- Vegetables with enough structure to hold together (corn, zucchini, peppers, eggplant)
- Flatbreads, pizza, and fruit (peaches, pineapple)

## Key Technique
1. **Preheat thoroughly** — 15–20 minutes with the lid closed. The grates should be too hot to hold your hand near for more than 2 seconds.
2. **Clean and oil the grates** — Brush with a grill brush, then oil with a folded paper towel dipped in high-smoke-point oil.
3. **Two-zone setup** — Pile coals on one side (or turn on only half the burners) for a hot sear zone and a cooler indirect zone.
4. **Don't press** — Pressing burgers or chicken squeezes out juices. Let the food sit until it releases naturally.
5. **Rest the meat** — Rest for 5–10 minutes after grilling. Internal temperature will rise 3–5°F during resting.

## Temperature Guide
- **High (230°C+ / 450°F+)** — Steaks, thin cuts, vegetables
- **Medium (175–230°C / 350–450°F)** — Chicken pieces, fish, sausages
- **Low indirect (120–150°C / 250–300°F)** — Whole chickens, ribs, large roasts

## Common Mistakes
- Opening the lid too often (loses heat and extends cooking time)
- Only using direct heat (thick cuts burn outside before cooking through)
- Not letting the grill preheat (food sticks and doesn't char properly)" } } } } }
turn.scope: req: Object {cuisineType, dishName, servings, dietaryRestri... cuisineType: string "italian" dishName: string "Creamy Tomato Basil Pasta" servings: number 4 dietaryRestriction: Array ["vegetarian"] cookingMethod: Array ["saute"] loaded: Object {cooking}

turn.message:
var cuisineType = (req?.cuisineType ?? "italian") as string var dishName = (req?.dishName ?? "Creamy Tomato Basil Pasta") as string var servings = Number(req?.servings ?? 4) || 4 var dietaryRestriction = (Array.isArray(req?.dietaryRestriction) ? req?.dietaryRestriction : [req?.dietaryRestriction ?? "vegetarian"]) as string[] var cookingMethod = (Array.isArray(req?.cookingMethod) ? req?.cookingMethod : [req?.cookingMethod ?? "saute"]) as string[]
var technique = (cookingMethod[0] ?? "saute") as string
var description = "Creamy, tomato-basil pasta with a silky sauce (no cream needed) and a fresh, vegetarian finish." var estimatedTime = "35–40 minutes" var difficulty = "Easy" var rationale = "Uses a quick sauté build for aromatics and a tomato-basil base; finishes with pasta water to emulsify into a creamy texture."
var baseIngredients = [ { item: "Dried pasta (penne, rigatoni, or fusilli)", qty: 400, unit: "g" }, { item: "Olive oil", qty: 2, unit: "tbsp" }, { item: "Onion", qty: 1, unit: "medium" }, { item: "Garlic", qty: 3, unit: "cloves" }, { item: "Crushed tomatoes (canned)", qty: 1, unit: "can (28 oz / ~800 g)" }, { item: "Tomato paste", qty: 1, unit: "tbsp" }, { item: "Fresh basil", qty: 1, unit: "packed cup" }, { item: "Vegetable broth", qty: 0.5, unit: "cup" }, { item: "Parmesan (or vegetarian hard cheese)", qty: 0.5, unit: "cup, grated" }, { item: "Salt", qty: 1, unit: "to taste" }, { item: "Black pepper", qty: 0.5, unit: "tsp" }, { item: "Red pepper flakes (optional)", qty: 0.25, unit: "tsp" }, ]
var scale = servings / 4 var scaledIngredients = baseIngredients.map((ing) => { var qtyNum = typeof ing.qty === "number" ? ing.qty * scale : ing.qty return { item: ing.item, qty: qtyNum, unit: ing.unit } })
var pantryAssumptions = [ "Salt and black pepper", "Red pepper flakes (optional)", "Vegetable broth (or use water + a bouillon cube)" ]
var advancePrep = [ "Chop onion and mince garlic.", "Grate parmesan (if using) and roughly chop basil leaves." ]
var immediatePrep = [ "Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil.", "Measure crushed tomatoes, tomato paste, and broth." ]
var equipmentNeeded = [ "Large pot", "Wide sauté pan or skillet", "Wooden spoon or spatula", "Knife and cutting board", "Colander" ]
var steps = [ "Cook pasta: Boil pasta in salted water until al dente. Reserve 1–1.5 cups of pasta water, then drain.", "Sauté aromatics: In a wide pan over medium-high heat, warm olive oil. Add diced onion and cook 4–5 minutes until softened. Add garlic and cook 30–60 seconds until fragrant.", "Build tomato base: Stir in tomato paste and cook 1 minute. Add crushed tomatoes and broth, scraping any browned bits. Simmer 8–10 minutes to thicken. Season with salt, pepper, and red pepper flakes (if using).", "Emulsify into creamy sauce: Add drained pasta to the pan. Toss well, adding reserved pasta water 1/4 cup at a time until glossy and creamy (usually 1/2–1 cup total).", "Finish: Turn off heat and stir in parmesan until melted. Add basil and toss just until wilted." ]
var totalActiveTime = "20–25 minutes active" var totalPassiveTime = "10–15 minutes simmering"
var ingredientsList = scaledIngredients.map((x) => { if (typeof x.qty === "number") { var rounded = x.unit === "g" ? Math.round(x.qty) : x.unit === "cup" || x.unit === "tbsp" ? Math.round(x.qty * 10) / 10 : Math.round(x.qty * 10) / 10 return `${rounded} ${x.unit} ${x.item}` } return `${x.qty} ${x.unit} ${x.item}` })
var methodText = (technique === "saute" ? "Sauté the aromatics first for flavor, then simmer the tomato sauce and emulsify with pasta water for a creamy finish." : "Use the selected technique to build flavor, then combine with pasta water to create a creamy emulsion." )
var perServingApprox = { calories: 520, protein_g: 18, carbs_g: 68, fat_g: 18, fiber_g: 6 }
var dietaryNotes = [ "Vegetarian (contains dairy if you use parmesan; omit for dairy-free).", "Gluten-free only if you use gluten-free pasta." ]
var nutrientSources = [ "Pasta (carbohydrates)", "Crushed tomatoes (fiber, antioxidants)", "Olive oil + parmesan (fats + some protein)", "Basil and onion/garlic (micronutrients)" ]
var timeToCook = "Total: ~35–40 minutes"
var recipeSteps = steps
display()
display(
Nutrition (approx per serving, varies by brands)
Calories: {perServingApprox.calories} kcal
Protein: {perServingApprox.protein_g} g • Carbs: {perServingApprox.carbs_g} g • Fat: {perServingApprox.fat_g} g • Fiber: {perServingApprox.fiber_g} g
Dietary notes {dietaryNotes.map((n) =>
• {n})} )
completeTask("recipe", "select-cuisine-and-dish", { cuisineType, dishName, description, servings, estimatedTime, difficulty, rationale }) completeTask("recipe", "gather-ingredients", { categories: ["produce", "pantry", "dairy/optional"], servingsBase: 4, pantryAssumptions }) completeTask("recipe", "prepare-mise-en-place", { advancePrep, immediatePrep, equipmentNeeded }) completeTask("recipe", "cook-step-by-step", { steps: recipeSteps, totalActiveTime, totalPassiveTime }) completeTask("recipe", "analyze-nutrition", { perServing: perServingApprox, nutrientSources, dietaryNotes: dietaryNotes })
turn.scope: req: Object {cuisineType, dishName, servings, dietaryRestri... cuisineType: string "italian" dishName: string "Creamy Tomato Basil Pasta" servings: number 4 dietaryRestriction: Array ["vegetarian"] cookingMethod: Array ["saute"] loaded: Object {cooking} technique: string "saute" description: string "Creamy, tomato-basil pasta with a silky sauce ... estimatedTime: string "35–40 minutes" difficulty: string "Easy" rationale: string "Uses a quick sauté build for aromatics and a t... baseIngredients: Array [{item, qty, unit}, {item, qty, unit}, {item, q... scale: number 1 scaledIngredients: Array [{item, qty, unit}, {item, qty, unit}, {item, q... pantryAssumptions: Array ["Salt and black p..., "Red pepper flake..., "V... advancePrep: Array ["Chop onion and m..., "Grate parmesan (...] immediatePrep: Array ["Bring a large po..., "Measure crushed ...] equipmentNeeded: Array ["Large pot", "Wide sauté pan o..., "Wooden spo... steps: Array ["Cook pasta: Boil..., "Sauté aromatics:..., "B... totalActiveTime: string "20–25 minutes active" totalPassiveTime: string "10–15 minutes simmering" ingredientsList: Array ["400 g Dried past..., "2 tbsp Olive oil", "1 m... methodText: string "Sauté the aromatics first for flavor, then sim... perServingApprox: Object {calories, protein_g, carbs_g, fat_g, fiber_g} dietaryNotes: Array ["Vegetarian (cont..., "Gluten-free only...] nutrientSources: Array ["Pasta (carbohydr..., "Crushed tomatoes..., "O... timeToCook: string "Total: ~35–40 minutes" recipeSteps: Array ["Cook pasta: Boil..., "Sauté aromatics:..., "B...
